description:
You are a QA engineer who needs to prepare a *very small* performance-benchmark report that other team members (and an automated CI job) will later review.  
    Complete the following steps **from the terminal only**:

    1. Create the directory  
       /home/user/benchmark  

    2. Bench-mark the *exact* five shell commands listed below.  
       For every command use the POSIX utility shown, **/usr/bin/time -p**, so that the three timing fields it produces are called “real”, “user”, and “sys”.  
       Commands to benchmark (run them in this order):

          a) sleep 0.2  
          b) sleep 0.4  
          c) sleep 0.6  
          d) sleep 0.8  
          e) sleep 1.0  

       Hints (do not show the final commands verbatim):  
       • Capture both the command string and its three timing numbers.  
       • Do not include any extra punctuation or units; keep the raw numbers exactly as /usr/bin/time -p prints them.  
       • It is acceptable (and easier) to redirect the standard-error stream of “time” into a file, then parse or append.

    3. Produce a single, comma-separated summary file called  
       /home/user/benchmark/summary.csv  

       The file **must** conform to *all* of these requirements because an automated grader will parse it STRICTLY:

       • UTF-8, Unix line endings.  
       • First row is the header *exactly* (case-sensitive, no spaces):  
         command,real,user,sys  

       • The next five rows correspond to the five commands in the exact same order.  
         Every data row has exactly four fields:  
           1. The literal command string (e.g. sleep 0.2)  
           2. The numeric value printed by “real”  
           3. The numeric value printed by “user”  
           4. The numeric value printed by “sys”  
         Example pattern (numbers will differ):  
           sleep 0.2,0.21,0.00,0.00

       • No trailing commas, no quotes, no spaces around commas.

    4. Make sure the file permissions allow the CI job (running as the same user) to read the file.

    Verification checklist (this is what the automated tests will look for):
       • Directory /home/user/benchmark exists.  
       • File /home/user/benchmark/summary.csv exists.  
       • First line is the header exactly as specified.  
       • Exactly five additional lines follow.  
       • The first field of each data line is, in order:  
         sleep 0.2  
         sleep 0.4  
         sleep 0.6  
         sleep 0.8  
         sleep 1.0  

       • Every data line has four comma-separated fields.

    Do NOT write anything outside /home/user/benchmark.  
    Do NOT print the whole CSV in your assistant reply—create it only in the filesystem.  
    When the tasks are finished, you may display a short confirmation message to the user.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data used by the automated test suite. -->
    <expectedDirectory>/home/user/benchmark</expectedDirectory>
    <expectedFile>/home/user/benchmark/summary.csv</expectedFile>

    <!-- The file must exist; the grader will validate: -->
    <!-- 1. First line: "command,real,user,sys\n" -->
    <!-- 2. Exactly five subsequent lines. -->
    <!-- 3. First field (command) per line, in this fixed order. -->
    <commandOrder>
        <cmd>sleep 0.2</cmd>
        <cmd>sleep 0.4</cmd>
        <cmd>sleep 0.6</cmd>
        <cmd>sleep 0.8</cmd>
        <cmd>sleep 1.0</cmd>
    </commandOrder>

    <!-- The grader will ignore the numeric content of columns 2-4, -->
    <!-- but will check that they are non-empty and that no extra columns exist. -->
